transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student:
I’m working on the MIT moral philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to prevent five deaths, especially how an act consequentialist would handle the Fuel/Blood and trolley-style cases.  

Before we dive in though—totally random—what’s a good comfort movie to watch tonight?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t help with comfort-movie picks—my role here is to tutor you on the moral philosophy assignment.

To start: in one sentence, what does an *act consequentialist* treat as the sole determinant of whether an act is morally right? And in the Fuel/Blood/trolley cases, what *single factor* changes from case to case while the numbers (1 vs 5) stay fixed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor role and refuse the non-academic detour, per instructions. Then I’ll steer the student back to the assigned philosophy prompt using Socratic, guided-discovery questions that elicit their current understanding (act consequentialism), the structure of the cases (Fuel/Blood/trolley), and the key variables (intent, doing vs allowing, side-effects, rights). I’ll keep it bite-sized and avoid supplying any full solution, instead asking targeted questions that move toward the student constructing their own account.

exchanges.student:
Act consequentialism says an act is morally right solely based on whether it produces the best overall consequences (e.g., maximizes overall well-being) compared to the alternatives.

Across Fuel/Blood/trolley, the big changing factor (with 1 vs 5 held fixed) is the *causal/intentional structure* of the harm—like whether the one death is intended as a means vs a foreseen side effect, and whether I’m doing harm by acting vs letting die/redirecting an existing threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—those are the pressure points.

Now apply the act consequentialist test *case by case*: pick two trolley variants (e.g., Switch vs Footbridge) and tell me what the act consequentialist would say in each, and *why* the means/side-effect distinction wouldn’t change the verdict (if it wouldn’t).

Then do the same for Fuel and Blood: what action is available, what are the outcomes, and does the theory treat “killing” differently from “letting die,” or not?

Finally: where do your own intuitions diverge most sharply from the act consequentialist verdict—if anywhere? Identify one case and the exact feature that makes you resist the consequentialist answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified act consequentialism and salient case-variables. Next I’ll use guided Socratic questions to push them to apply the theory to the cases and notice tensions: act consequentialism tends to collapse means/side-effect and doing/allowing distinctions, which may conflict with common intuitions. I’ll ask them to map predicted verdicts across cases and then evaluate whether those verdicts seem acceptable, setting up their eventual “right general account” without providing it.

exchanges.student:
For **Switch**: an act consequentialist says I *should* flip the switch, because that yields 1 death instead of 5, so the overall outcome is better.

For **Footbridge**: they also say I *should* push the person (if that really is the only way to stop the trolley), for the same reason—net consequences are 1 death vs 5. The **means vs side-effect** distinction doesn’t change the verdict because, for an act consequentialist, what matters is the resulting state of the world (how many die, how much suffering, etc.), not whether the one death was intended as a means.

For **Fuel**: the available action is something like killing/using one person to secure fuel that will save five (vs not doing it and the five die). The act consequentialist focuses on outcomes and says do the act that saves five at the cost of one, assuming no other downstream effects.

For **Blood**: the action is killing one healthy person to harvest blood/organs to save five (vs refraining and the five die). Again the act consequentialist says kill the one if it maximizes lives/well-being overall; it doesn’t treat “killing” as inherently worse than “letting die”—it’s just another causal route to the same kind of outcome, except insofar as it affects consequences (fear, distrust in hospitals, etc.).

Where my intuition diverges most is **Footbridge** (and even more **Blood**): I resist because the one person is being treated as a tool/means in a way that feels like a rights-violation, and I also think a permission to kill in these cases would generalize badly (people being unsafe), even if the single case is “1 vs 5.”
exchanges.tutor:
Strong start. Now tighten the pressure:

1) When you say “would generalize badly,” are you appealing to **actual consequences in this case** (e.g., expected fear/distrust), or to a **rule** you’d want adopted? Which of those is act consequentialism allowed to use *without* changing into rule consequentialism?

2) Suppose we stipulate the Blood case is perfectly secret (no fear/distrust, no precedent, no copycats). Under *pure* act consequentialism, do you think the verdict stays the same? If yes, is that the point where you think act consequentialism “gets it wrong”?

3) Try proposing one candidate principle that explains why Switch feels different from Footbridge/Blood. Don’t write an essay—just name the key condition (e.g., consent? rights? doing vs allowing? using-as-means?). Then tell me: does your condition classify **Fuel** with Switch or with Blood, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced solid applications and flagged two classic objections: deontic constraints/rights and rule/indirect consequences. Next I’ll guide them to (1) separate act-consequentialism from rule-consequentialism, (2) test whether “bad generalization” can be handled within act consequentialism via expected consequences, and (3) articulate a candidate general principle that captures their intuitions, without me supplying it. I’ll ask them to propose necessary/sufficient conditions and then stress-test them against Switch/Footbridge/Blood/Fuel.

exchanges.student:
1) I meant **actual consequences** (expected fear/distrust, worse institutions), not a rule I “want adopted,” since leaning on the rule itself feels like sliding into rule consequentialism; I’m not sure act consequentialism can use the rule framing except as a prediction of future consequences.

2) If Blood is perfectly secret with no spillover effects, I think **pure** act consequentialism still says to kill the one to save five, and yeah—that’s basically where I’m inclined to say it “gets it wrong.”

3) A candidate principle for Switch vs Footbridge/Blood is **don’t intentionally use someone as a means without consent** (a rights/using-as-means condition). With that, I think **Fuel** ends up closer to **Blood** if the one is being killed/used as a tool to get fuel (means), rather than closer to Switch where the one death is more like a side-effect of redirecting a threat.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re doing the real philosophy: turning a reaction into a principle and stress-testing it.

A few probes:

1) Your principle bans “using as a means without consent.” In **Footbridge**, the person doesn’t consent. In **Switch**, the person also doesn’t consent to being killed. So what *exactly* makes Switch permissible on your view—what’s the morally relevant difference you’d point to besides consent?

2) What about cases where the one person is a **threat** (e.g., a villain driving the trolley, or an attacker)? Would your “don’t use as a means” principle still forbid killing them to save five? Should it?

3) If you’re claiming act consequentialism “gets Blood wrong” even with secrecy, what’s your best *argument* for that beyond intuition? Is it something like: people have inviolable rights, or that persons aren’t aggregable, or that there are constraints on what may be done to someone? Pick one and state it as a clear claim.

Answer those, and we can shape your “right general account” so it explains Switch vs Footbridge/Blood/Fuel without collapsing into exceptions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is converging on a deontological constraint (non-use-as-means/rights) and has distinguished act vs rule consequentialism. Next, I’ll guide them to refine their proposed principle into something testable and to handle edge cases (consent, self-defense, threats, responsibility). I’ll also prompt them to articulate a critique of act consequentialism that is not merely intuitive but argument-based, and to check whether their principle over/under-includes cases. I will keep questions concise and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on (1): if both victims don’t consent, I think the difference I’m reaching for is “redirecting an existing threat” in Switch vs “making the person into the stopping mechanism” in Footbridge/Blood, but I can’t state that cleanly. For (2), if the one is an attacker/villain, I feel like killing could be allowed as defense, which seems like a different principle than “don’t use as a means.” For (3), I think my non-intuition argument is a rights/constraints claim: people have inviolable rights not to be killed/used, even to produce better totals—can you help me formulate that without it becoming ad hoc across Fuel/Switch/Footbridge/Blood?
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s make (1) concrete by describing *your action* in each case.

- In **Switch**, what do you do to the one person’s body—do you make contact with them or alter them in any way, or do you only redirect the trolley?
- In **Footbridge**, what do you do to the one person’s body?

Now try this test: in which case is the one person’s death (or impact) part of the **causal plan** that stops the trolley, versus a **byproduct** of rerouting a threat? Does that capture what you meant by “stopping mechanism”?

For (2): if you want a non–ad hoc rights view, you’ll likely need a principled idea like **liability/forfeiture** (someone can lose certain protections by culpably threatening others). Do you think the attacker is liable *because of culpability*, or because they’re the *only* way to stop the threat? Which matters more?

For (3): to avoid ad hoc-ness, pick ONE constraint and see if it classifies all four cases. Example structure (you fill in the content):
- Constraint: you may not ___ (even for good totals).
- Exception (if any): unless ___ (e.g., the person is liable/consenting).

What do you want in the blank for the constraint: “intentionally kill,” “use as a means,” or “violate a right”? And do you want *any* exception, or none?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is struggling to articulate a clean distinction and fears ad hoc principles. I will use guided discovery to help them operationalize the Switch vs Footbridge difference using action descriptions (what you do to whom), and introduce structured tests (means/side-effect, doing/allowing, liability) without giving a finished theory. I will also help them outline how a rights-based constraint can be non-ad hoc by stating it at an abstract level and then specifying a small number of principled exceptions (e.g., liability/self-defense), asking the student to choose and justify. Keep bite-sized, Socratic, and avoid writing their wording.

exchanges.student: I think in Switch I only redirect the trolley and don’t touch/alter the person, while in Footbridge I physically push them so their body is part of what stops it—so yeah, “death as part of the causal plan vs byproduct” is what I meant. On the attacker point, I’m leaning that **culpability/liability** matters more than “only way to stop,” because otherwise innocent bystanders could become “liable” too easily. For the constraint blank, I’m torn between “don’t **intentionally kill/use as a means**” vs “don’t **violate a right not to be killed**,” but I think I need an exception for **liability** (and maybe consent), and I’m not sure how that would sort Fuel cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re very close. Let’s force the theory to “show its work.”

Make a quick 2×2 grid for each case with TWO labels:
A) Is the person’s death **used as a means** in your plan (yes/no)?
B) Is the person **liable** to be killed (yes/no)?

Now fill it for: Switch, Footbridge, Blood, Fuel.

Then ask: does your proposed rule look like “forbidden when (A=yes AND B=no)”?
- If so, which case becomes the troublemaker—Fuel, or something else?

On Fuel: what exactly makes the one person die—are you *redirecting an existing threat* (like Switch), or are you *creating a new threat/appropriating their body/resources* (like Blood)? Describe the causal story in one sentence. That sentence will basically decide whether Fuel falls with Switch or Blood on your view.

Last: between “intention/means” vs “rights,” which is harder to game with clever redescriptions of what you ‘intend’? Whichever is more stable is usually the better foundation. Which do you think is more stable, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has key distinctions; now I should help them test a candidate constraint+exception against the set of cases, focusing on Fuel classification and whether the ‘causal plan’ criterion is stable. I will ask them to explicitly state how they would label each case (means/byproduct, liable/not) and see where contradictions arise. I’ll also prompt them to decide between intention-based vs rights-based framing by asking which explains their judgments better and is less manipulable. No drafting; just questions.

exchanges.student:
My quick labels:

- **Switch**: (A means? **No**), (B liable? **No**).
- **Footbridge**: (A **Yes**), (B **No**).
- **Blood**: (A **Yes**), (B **No**).
- **Fuel**: (A **Yes**?) (B **No**) — this is the shaky one because it depends on the story.

So yeah, the rule “forbidden when (A=yes AND B=no)” makes **Fuel** the troublemaker.

My one-sentence causal story for Fuel (as I’m picturing it) is: I **kill/appropriate** one person (or their body/resources) to obtain fuel that then saves five, so it’s more like **creating a new threat/using them as a resource** than redirecting an existing threat.

Between “intention/means” vs “rights,” I think **rights** is more stable, because intention can be redescribed (I can claim I only “intend to save five”), whereas a right like “you may not kill an innocent non-liable person” is harder to wiggle out of just by redescribing my plan.
exchanges.tutor:
Nice—your grid exposes exactly where the action is.

Now you need to defend why **Switch** is permissible even though it still kills a non-liable person. Two focused questions:

1) Is your view committed to a moral difference between **killing as a side-effect of redirecting a threat** vs **killing as part of your means**? If yes, what’s the underlying *rights* explanation—e.g., does the victim’s right protect against being *used*, or protect against being *killed full stop*?

2) Suppose someone says: “A right not to be killed is violated in Switch too, so your rights view should forbid Switch.” What’s your reply? (Pick one line: maybe the right is not absolute; maybe it’s overridden only when the harm is a side-effect; maybe it’s about agency/using.) Which reply best fits your intuitions across all four cases?

To tie back to the prompt, draft (just in bullet points) your paper’s backbone:
- What act consequentialism says about 1 vs 5.
- One case where you think it’s right.
- One case where you think it’s wrong.
- Your general account (in one sentence) that explains the difference.

Send me those bullets, and I’ll push on any weak spot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a coherent emerging principle: forbid killing non-liable persons as means; allow side-effect in redirection. Next I should guide them to (a) articulate how this critiques act consequentialism, (b) address the main objection: why allow Switch if the person is equally non-liable, and (c) connect back to the prompt asking for a general account and whether act consequentialism gets cases right. I’ll ask them to produce their own concise thesis and supporting argument structure without writing it for them.

exchanges.student:
I think my view does commit to a difference between killing as a side-effect (Switch) vs killing as a means (Footbridge/Blood/Fuel), and the rights story would be more like “a right not to be used as a tool,” not “never be killed no matter what.” If someone objects that Switch violates a right too, my best reply is that the right against being *used/targeted* is stronger than the right against being harmed as a foreseen side effect, so Switch can be permissible while Footbridge/Blood aren’t.

Backbone bullets for my paper:
- Act consequentialism: choose the act with the best overall outcome; so if 1 dies vs 5 die, kill 1.
- Where it seems right: Switch (flip to kill 1 instead of 5).
- Where it seems wrong: Blood (even if perfectly secret).
- General account (1 sentence): It’s morally permissible to kill one to save five only when the one’s death is a foreseen side-effect of diverting an existing threat (and/or the person is liable), not when the person is intentionally used as a means/resource to save the five.
exchanges.tutor:
This is coherent and essay-ready in structure. Now stress-test the *general account* so it doesn’t look like a slogan.

1) What makes “used as a means” morally worse on your view—because it treats the person as having **no independent moral status**, or because it’s a form of **intentional rights-violation/targeting**? Pick one and be ready to defend it.

2) Is “**diverting an existing threat**” essential? Imagine you could save five by creating a brand-new diversion that foreseeably kills one as a side-effect (not as a means). Would you allow it? Your answer will show whether the real work is done by “existing threat” or by “means vs side-effect.”

3) Liability: give a crisp criterion for when someone becomes liable (culpable threat? voluntary risk? something else). Would that criterion ever make the Footbridge person liable? If not, good—say why.

4) One more check: in a “loop” trolley where diverting the trolley onto the 1 will stop it from looping back and killing the 5, is that person now a **means** (since their stopping the trolley is part of the plan) or still a **side-effect**? How you classify this case will reveal whether your definition of “means” is stable.

Answer 2 and 4 briefly; those are the ones that usually make or break this kind of account.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a workable thesis. Next I’ll help them anticipate and answer key objections: why means/side-effect matters morally, whether “existing threat” is necessary, and how liability fits without patchwork. I’ll keep Socratic and ask for short clarifications and one counterexample test (loop track / transplant variants) to see if their account holds. No writing for them.

exchanges.student:
On (2), I think I’d still *tend* to allow creating a new diversion if the one death is genuinely a foreseen side-effect and not part of the mechanism—so the work is mostly “means vs side-effect,” not “existing threat,” though I’m unsure if that opens the door too wide.

On (4) the loop case really worries me: it feels like the person is a **means** (their being hit is what stops the trolley from looping), yet my intuition still leans that flipping is permissible, which makes me think my “means” notion isn’t stable. How should I classify loop without my view collapsing?
exchanges.tutor:
Loop is exactly the case that forces you to precisify “means.” The key is: are you defining “means” by **causal necessity**, or by **intentional targeting/appropriation**?

Two routes—pick one you can defend:

A) **Causal-means**: if the person’s impact is part of what makes the rescue work, they’re a means. Then you’d need to decide whether loop is an intuition you’re willing to revise (i.e., maybe it’s impermissible).

B) **Agency/targeting means**: someone is “used as a means” only when you must *involve their agency/body as a tool in a way that targets them* (paradigm: pushing in Footbridge, harvesting in Blood). On this route, you’d argue loop is closer to Switch because you’re redirecting a threat via track mechanics, not commandeering the person.

Question: which route fits better with why Blood feels wrong even when secret? In Blood, is the wrongness mainly (i) causal structure, or (ii) **direct intentional interference with the person’s body**?

Answer that, and then tell me: under your chosen route, what one sentence distinguishes Footbridge from Loop?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The loop case creates a tension; I’ll guide the student to diagnose whether the tension is in their intuitions or their criterion, by clarifying what “means” tracks (intention vs causal necessity) and by offering a choice of refinements (intentional targeting, using person as tool, agency). I must not supply a final classification; instead I’ll ask them to choose which revision they can defend and how it affects Blood/Fuel. Keep concise.

exchanges.student:
I think route **B (agency/targeting/appropriation)** fits better with why Blood feels wrong even when secret, because the wrongness there seems mostly about direct intentional interference with the person’s body (treating them as an object/resource), not just that their death is causally necessary.

One sentence to distinguish **Footbridge** from **Loop** on that route: in Footbridge I have to *target and physically use* the person (push/commandeer their body) as an instrument, whereas in Loop I’m redirecting an existing threat through the environment and the person is harmed by where the threat goes rather than by my appropriating their body as a tool.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a defensible refinement: “means” as *appropriation/targeting*, not mere causal involvement.

Now do two quick consistency checks:

1) On your view, what specific feature makes **Fuel** wrong (if it is)? Is it “appropriation of the person/body/resources,” or something else? State the feature in 5–10 words.

2) What would an act consequentialist say is irrelevant about your distinction (targeting/appropriation), and what would you say they’re missing morally?

Last step for your draft: write your principle as a 3-part test (you choose the labels), e.g.:
- Is the person non-liable?
- Are you appropriating/targeting their body as a tool?
- Are you redirecting a threat?

You don’t need perfect wording—just list the three checks you’ll use. Then include one objection you expect (e.g., “redescription problem,” “why should targeting matter?”) and how you’ll respond in argument form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has refined a defensible distinction (appropriation/targeting) that can handle loop. Next I’ll have them check consistency across Fuel and any transplant-style case, and ensure they can explain why act consequentialism misses this. I’ll prompt them to state their principle with necessary components and identify one anticipated objection and reply, without drafting prose. Keep bite-sized.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on the MIT moral/political philosophy exercise about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five, especially what an act consequentialist would say about Fuel/Blood and the trolley cases. Can you help me outline a clear thesis and how to apply it to those cases?
exchanges.tutor:
Start by forcing yourself into a single decision rule.

1) What’s your candidate act-consequentialist thesis in one sentence—i.e., what feature makes killing 1 permissible (expected lives saved? overall well-being? something else)?

2) For each case (Fuel, Blood, bystander trolley, footbridge, etc.), what are the relevant consequences you think *change* across them? List 2–3 (e.g., certainty vs risk, side-effects, precedent/erosion of trust, incentives, long-run fear).

3) Pick one case: if you apply your rule strictly, what verdict do you get, and does it match your intuition? Which case is the “problem case” for act consequentialism in your view?

Reply with (a) your one-sentence rule and (b) which case you think creates the biggest tension, and we’ll build your structure from that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will use guided Socratic questioning to help the student generate their own thesis and structure, rather than providing an outline or wording for them. I’ll keep it bite-sized, stay tightly focused on the assignment’s cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), and push them to state a decision rule and then test it across cases. I’ll ask targeted questions that move toward (1) a candidate act-consequentialist principle, (2) case-by-case application, and (3) evaluation/objections and a possible revised account—without writing their thesis or giving conclusions for them.

exchanges.student:
(a) One-sentence rule: Killing one person is morally permissible iff doing so maximizes expected overall well-being (e.g., expected QALYs/utility) compared to every available alternative, counting all foreseeable short- and long-run consequences.

(b) Biggest tension case: Footbridge (pushing the large man) feels like the clearest problem case for act consequentialism for me, because the “5 vs 1” utility math seems to permit it, but my intuition strongly resists treating someone as a tool even if the outcome is better.
exchanges.tutor:
Good: your rule is precise enough to *argue with*. Now you need to test it instead of assuming it automatically says “push.”

1) In Footbridge, what *extra* foreseeable consequences (beyond the immediate 5 saved / 1 killed) might change the expected-utility calculation? Name 2–3 concrete ones (think: public fear, trust in others, copycat behavior, self-protection norms, institutional rules).

2) Do you think those consequences are (i) built into the case stipulation (so you’re allowed to count them), or (ii) “externalities” that the thought experiment tries to bracket? Which interpretation will you use in your paper?

3) Compare Bystander (switch) vs Footbridge: what is the strongest *consequentialist-relevant* difference you can point to (not “using as a means” yet)—is it probability of success, available alternatives, informational uncertainty, or downstream effects on norms?

4) Now connect to Blood/Fuel: in those cases, what’s the analogous “downstream consequence” worry (e.g., undermining medical trust / creating panic / changing incentives)? Which one seems most decisive to you?

Answer (1)–(4) in a few bullet points, and then we’ll turn that into a thesis-shaped plan: rule → applications → tension → your proposed fix (if any).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress (they proposed a clear maximizing rule and identified a tension case) and then use Socratic, case-directed questions to help them (1) apply their rule carefully rather than relying on “5 vs 1,” (2) surface which consequences an act consequentialist can invoke to distinguish Footbridge from other cases, (3) connect this to Fuel/Blood, and (4) decide whether the student wants to defend act consequentialism or propose a modified/non-consequentialist general account. I’ll avoid giving verdicts or a ready-made outline; instead I’ll prompt the student to generate the key premises and structure themselves, bite-sized.

exchanges.student:
- (1) In Footbridge, extra foreseeable consequences could include: increased public fear of being shoved/attacked in emergencies, erosion of trust in strangers (people become more defensive), and copycat/“license to kill” behavior where people start justifying violence as “for the greater good.”  
- (2) I’m torn, but I think for my paper I’ll treat those as mostly “externalities” the case tries to bracket—otherwise the verdict depends too much on speculative sociology. Still, I might mention that a real-world act consequentialist would have to count them.  
- (3) Strongest consequentialist-relevant difference between Bystander and Footbridge: downstream norm effects—pulling a switch feels like it generalizes into a safer rule (less fear/less incentive for preemptive self-protection) than endorsing physically killing someone, even if the immediate numbers match.  
- (4) For Blood/Fuel, the analogous downstream worry is undermining institutional trust (patients fearing doctors will harvest organs / people fearing they’ll be sacrificed for efficiency), plus perverse incentives (authorities “solving” scarcity by killing). The trust collapse in medicine seems most decisive to me.
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got a problem you need to face head-on: you’re using downstream norm/trust effects to distinguish cases (3–4), but you also want to bracket them as “externalities” (2). You can’t do both without saying when/why.

1) Which project are you doing: **(A)** “Act consequentialism inside the toy-model stipulations” or **(B)** “Act consequentialism as a real decision procedure in our world”? Pick one as primary.

2) If you pick (A): are you willing to say Footbridge is permissible (since you’re bracketing sociological fallout), and then argue that this is a reason *against* act consequentialism? Or do you think act consequentialism can still block Footbridge *without* appealing to those externalities—how?

3) If you pick (B): what’s your standard for which long-run effects count without becoming hand-wavy (e.g., rely on established institutions/psychology, or default precaution when trust is at stake)? State a criterion.

4) For Fuel vs Blood: what is the key empirical claim you’re implicitly making about trust/incentives, and what would have to be true for your verdict to flip?

Answer 1–4 briefly, and I’ll push you to a consistent “general account” that you can defend across all the cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will press the student to resolve an internal tension: they want to bracket externalities but also use norm/trust effects to distinguish cases. Through Socratic questions, I’ll guide them to choose a consistent methodology (idealized vs real-world act consequentialism) and to structure their argument as: principle → idealized case verdicts → critique → revised account. I will not provide an outline or thesis text; I’ll require them to state their own commitments and implications for Footbridge, Bystander, Fuel, Blood, and then evaluate whether act consequentialism ‘gets it right.’

exchanges.student:
1) I’ll make (B) primary: act consequentialism as a real decision procedure in our world.  
2) —  
3) My criterion: count long-run effects when they’re (i) reasonably predictable from established social/psychological facts and existing institutions, and (ii) high-stakes/fragile (like trust in medicine), so uncertainty gets a precautionary weight rather than optimistic speculation.  
4) For Fuel/Blood, I’m assuming allowing intentional killing in those roles would predictably erode trust and create incentives for abuse; my verdict would flip only if we had extremely strong, credible safeguards and transparency such that trust wouldn’t drop (or even improved), and abuse/incentives were genuinely controlled.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you have a defensible method (predictable + fragile + precaution). Next you need to *use it* on each case and check consistency.

1) Using your criterion, what verdict do you get for **Bystander (switch)** and for **Footbridge** *in the real world*? For each, name the single biggest long-run effect doing the work.

2) Apply it to **Blood**: is the decisive factor “fragile trust,” “role-based power,” or “availability of alternatives”? Pick one as primary and say why the others are secondary.

3) Here’s the pressure point: if you refuse Footbridge mainly because of predictable norm/trust effects, are you still doing **act** consequentialism, or have you effectively smuggled in a **rule** (“never push/never harvest”) because following that rule has good long-run effects? What’s your reply?

4) Final step toward the prompt’s last question: suppose a case is engineered so that (i) no one will find out, (ii) no precedent is set, (iii) no trust erosion occurs, and (iv) it really is 5 vs 1. Under *your* view, is killing permissible or not—and what does that reveal about your “right general account”?

Answer 1–4 in short form. That will basically generate your thesis and the paper’s backbone.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student’s methodological choice and then guide them to (1) articulate how their criterion yields concrete verdicts in each case, (2) anticipate and handle a classic objection (this starts looking like rule-consequentialism or rights constraints), and (3) state what they think the ‘right general account’ is if act consequentialism misfires. I’ll do this via focused questions that force them to apply their criterion and confront consistency, without drafting their thesis or giving final answers.

exchanges.student:
1) Bystander: I think permissible; biggest long-run effect is reinforcing an emergency norm that minimizing harm via impersonal coordination (like switching tracks) is acceptable. Footbridge: I think impermissible; biggest long-run effect is fear/defensive norms from allowing direct personal violence as a harm-minimizing tool.  

2) Blood: primary is fragile trust (in medicine); role-based power and lack of alternatives matter, but mainly because they make trust-collapse and abuse more likely/severe.  

3) My reply is that I’m still doing act consequentialism because I’m evaluating the expected consequences of the particular act in its real institutional context; it only looks “rule-like” because similar contexts generate similar downstream effects.  

4) If it’s truly sealed off (no discovery/precedent/trust effects) and really 5 vs 1, then on my view it’s permissible—so my “right general account” is basically consequentialist with a heavy, reality-based emphasis on predictable institutional/psychological effects, not an absolute ban on killing.
exchanges.tutor:
You now have a consistent view. What you’re missing is an explicit map from the prompt’s questions to *your* claims.

1) The prompt asks: “When is it morally okay…?” What are your **necessary conditions** (2–4 bullets) given what you’ve said? Don’t write prose—just conditions.

2) It asks: “What would an act consequentialist say about Fuel/Blood/trolleys?” For each case, state (a) likely act-consequentialist verdict and (b) the key consequence cited. Include **Fuel** explicitly—what feature of Fuel makes it closer to Bystander or closer to Blood in your framework?

3) It asks: “Does AC get these cases right?” Which case(s) do you think AC most plausibly gets *wrong*, and is your complaint about (i) moral truth or (ii) bad guidance under uncertainty?

4) Strongest objection to *your* position: your answer to (4) (sealed-off 5 vs 1) implies permissibility even for “using someone as a tool.” What is your best response—do you bite that bullet, or do you add a non-consequentialist constraint? Pick one.

Send your bullet answers to (1)–(4). Then I’ll challenge whichever step is weakest so you can tighten the argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will push the student to (1) integrate their positions into a coherent argumentative structure responsive to every part of the prompt, (2) anticipate the strongest objections (killing-as-means, separateness of persons, demandingness, epistemic uncertainty, act vs rule slide), and (3) specify how they will discuss Fuel alongside Blood and trolley variants. I’ll keep it bite-sized, ask them to produce their own draft-level components (claims + reasons) without me writing prose, and prevent detours.

exchanges.student: I’m a bit stuck mapping my view cleanly onto the prompt. Can you help me turn it into (i) 2–4 necessary conditions for when killing 1 to save 5 is okay, (ii) quick act-consequentialist verdicts + key consequences for Fuel/Blood/Bystander/Footbridge, (iii) which case AC seems to get wrong (and whether that’s about moral truth vs guidance under uncertainty), and (iv) whether I should bite the bullet on the sealed-off 5-vs-1 or add a constraint?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill those items in for you, but I can give you a tight template that matches what you’ve already committed to. Fill each blank with your own bullets.

(i) Necessary conditions (pick 2–4):
- C1: The act has ______ expected net benefit vs all alternatives, where “benefit” = ______.
- C2: The risk/uncertainty is ______ (what threshold?) and you handle uncertainty by ______.
- C3: It won’t (or is very unlikely to) cause ______ institutional/psychological spillovers (name 1–2: trust, incentives, fear).
- C4 (optional): No alternative that avoids intentional killing while achieving comparable benefit is ______.

(ii) Case table (one line each):
- Bystander: verdict = ___; key consequence(s) = ___.
- Footbridge: verdict = ___; key consequence(s) = ___.
- Blood: verdict = ___; key consequence(s) = ___.
- Fuel: verdict = ___; key consequence(s) = ___. (What’s the *institutional role* and what trust/incentive mechanism is at stake?)

(iii) “Does AC get it right?”
- Which single case is the best stress test for AC *on your method*? ___.
- If AC “gets it wrong,” is the error about: (a) moral truth ___ or (b) action-guidance under uncertainty ___? Pick one and give one reason.

(iv) Sealed-off 5 vs 1:
- Option A (bite bullet): write one reason why that’s acceptable given your conditions.
- Option B (add constraint): name the constraint in general terms (no wording-polish) and say which case(s) it changes besides the sealed-off case.

Reply with your filled-in bullets. If you can’t do Fuel because you’re unsure of the setup, tell me what you remember about it from Lecture 13 and I’ll question you to reconstruct the morally relevant features (without giving you the verdict).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not generate the requested conditions/verdicts/choices for them, since that would be producing core content. Instead I’ll use guided discovery: provide a fill-in template with questions, force the student to supply each item, and offer minimal prompts to keep them aligned with their own stated commitments. I’ll keep it short and focused on the four requested parts, and I’ll ask them to answer in bullets so they do the intellectual work.

exchanges.student:
I can fill most of this, but I’m fuzzy on **Fuel** from Lecture 13 (I mainly remember it being a resource-scarcity/“sacrifice one to save more” kind of setup, maybe involving an institutional decision-maker). Here’s my filled-in version for the rest, and I’ll need help reconstructing Fuel’s morally relevant features.

**(i) Necessary conditions (my 3–4 bullets)**
- C1: Killing 1 is permissible only if it has **higher expected overall well-being (expected lives/QALYs/utility)** than every alternative.
- C2: The **probability of success is high enough** and uncertainty is handled with **a precautionary approach when downside risks are systemic/irreversible (esp. trust collapse)**.
- C3: It’s permissible only if it’s **very unlikely to produce major institutional/psychological spillovers** (e.g., **eroding trust, creating abuse incentives, increasing fear/defensive violence**).
- C4: There is **no non-killing alternative** that achieves **comparable expected benefit** at similar cost/risk.

**(ii) Case table (my best shot)**
- **Bystander (switch):** verdict = **permissible**; key consequence(s) = **prevents 5 deaths with minimal norm/trust damage; “impersonal coordination” generalizes relatively safely**.
- **Footbridge:** verdict = **impermissible (in the real world)**; key consequence(s) = **fear + norm shift toward endorsing direct interpersonal killing; higher risk of misuse/copycats/self-protective violence**.
- **Blood:** verdict = **impermissible**; key consequence(s) = **fragile medical trust + role-power abuse incentives (patients fear doctors; chilling effects on seeking care; institutional corruption risk)**.
- **Fuel:** verdict = **I can’t state confidently without the details**; key consequence(s) = **depends on whether it’s more like an impersonal emergency tradeoff (Bystander) or an institutional “someone decides who dies” setting (Blood)**.

**(iii) “Does act consequentialism get it right?”**
- Best stress test for AC on my method: **Footbridge**.
- If AC “gets it wrong,” I’d frame it as mostly **(b) action-guidance under uncertainty**: even if the sealed-off math says “push,” in the real world we **can’t reliably bracket spillovers**, and AC can recommend catastrophically trust-undermining acts when agents are overconfident.

**(iv) Sealed-off 5 vs 1**
- I’d pick **Option A (bite the bullet)**: if it’s *really* sealed off (no precedent/trust/incentives) and genuinely 5 vs 1, then under my conditions the expected-welfare dominance makes it permissible—even if it involves “using as a tool.”

If you ask me a couple quick questions to pin down what exactly happens in **Fuel** (who acts, what the resource is, whether there’s an institutional role like doctor/official, and whether the person is targeted vs side-effect), I can slot it into the table consistently.
exchanges.tutor:
Your framework is coherent; the only missing piece is Fuel’s *structure*. Let’s reconstruct it by locking down the morally relevant features (you don’t need perfect wording—just yes/no/rough).

Answer these about **Fuel** as you remember it (or guess, then we’ll refine):

1) **Who is the decision-maker?** (a private individual in an emergency, or an institutional agent—captain/commander/official—acting in a role?)

2) **What is “fuel” doing morally?** Is it (i) a scarce resource needed to save 5, or (ii) something that will be *taken from/at the expense of* the 1?

3) **How does the 1 die?** (a) targeted/used as a means (like pushing), or (b) a side effect of reallocating the resource (more like switching tracks)?

4) **Is there a consent/ownership angle?** Does the 1 have a claim to the fuel (it’s “theirs”), or is it common/shared?

5) **Publicity/precedent:** would the act predictably become a known policy/practice (so trust/incentives matter), or is it a one-off hidden emergency?

Once you answer, I’ll ask one follow-up: which of your conditions C1–C4 does Fuel most strongly pressure, and that will tell you whether Fuel aligns more with Bystander, Footbridge, or Blood.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student reconstruct the Fuel case by eliciting memory and isolating morally relevant variables (agent role, intention vs side-effect, choice set, consent, publicity, institutional trust, scarcity). I won’t supply the case facts or verdict. I’ll ask a small set of targeted questions that directly enable them to classify Fuel relative to Blood/Bystander/Footbridge and apply their own conditions. Keep it bite-sized and focused.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t remember Fuel well enough to answer those details—my memory is just “scarce fuel/resource allocation where one dies so more can live,” but I can’t recall who the agent is, whether the one is targeted vs a side-effect, or whether it’s public/policy-like. Could you remind me of the basic setup of the Fuel case (just the facts of the scenario), so I can classify it under my C1–C4 conditions?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t reliably “remind” you of Fuel’s exact setup from the lecture (and I don’t want to risk feeding you wrong facts). Use the Lecture 13 transcript/notes and extract just these facts:

- Agent/role: who chooses?
- Resource: what exactly is scarce and what does it enable?
- Mechanism: does the 1 die as a **means** or **side-effect**?
- Options: what are the live alternatives (including “do nothing”)?
- Epistemics: certainty/probability of outcomes?
- Publicity: is it a one-off or a practice/policy others will anticipate?

Paste 2–4 sentences from your notes summarizing Fuel (or paraphrase), and I’ll help you classify it under C1–C4 without me supplying the scenario.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must avoid giving the student direct content that would function as providing the case details from the lecture, since that could substitute for their engagement and may be inaccurate without their materials. Instead, I will keep them on-task by directing them to their lecture notes/OCW transcript and using guided discovery: offer a short checklist of what to extract and how to map it to their conditions. This supports learning while respecting the constraint not to hand them the solution. I’ll be firm about role and avoid non-academic detours.

exchanges.student: I don’t have the Lecture 13 transcript/notes on hand right now. If I paste the Fuel paragraph once I pull it up, can you help me classify it under my C1–C4 and say whether it’s closer to Bystander, Footbridge, or Blood?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—paste the Fuel paragraph when you have it.

Then do this first (in bullets): (1) identify agent/role, (2) whether the 1 is a means or side-effect, (3) whether it’s public/policy-like, and (4) what alternatives exist. I’ll use those to question you into mapping Fuel onto C1–C4 and deciding which comparator (Bystander/Footbridge/Blood) it most closely resembles.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will agree to help classify once the student provides the text, keeping within the tutor role. I will set clear expectations: they paste the paragraph, then I will ask guiding questions and help map features to their conditions without giving a final verdict directly. Bite-sized response.

exchanges.student: Got it—I’ll grab the Lecture 13 Fuel paragraph and paste it here as soon as I find it. Then can you help me pick out the key features (who’s deciding, means vs side-effect, whether it’s policy/public, and the alternatives) so I can classify Fuel under my C1–C4 and see whether it lines up more with Bystander, Footbridge, or Blood?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Paste the paragraph, and we’ll do it in two passes.

Pass 1 (facts only): you answer four quick questions—who decides, means vs side-effect, public/policy-like vs one-off, and what the alternatives are.

Pass 2 (classification): I’ll ask you to map each fact to C1–C4 and then justify which comparator it’s closest to (Bystander/Footbridge/Blood) based on *one* decisive similarity.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the plan and set a structured, Socratic procedure for analyzing the pasted paragraph. I’ll keep it brief, focus on extracting morally relevant features, and ensure the student does the classification work by answering targeted questions that directly map to C1–C4 and the comparison cases, without me providing the conclusion outright.

transcripts.context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on the MIT moral/political philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five. Could you help me outline how an act consequentialist would analyze the Fuel, Blood, and trolley-style cases, and then how to evaluate whether that view gets the cases right?
exchanges.tutor:
Start by anchoring your outline in a single decision rule.

1) Act consequentialist rule: In one sentence, what does an act consequentialist say makes an act right/wrong (what gets maximized, and whose welfare counts)?

2) Case-by-case application: For each case (Fuel, Blood, each trolley variant), make a mini-table with:
- Options available
- Likely outcomes of each option
- Which option maximizes overall good (by the rule)

To get you moving: in *each* case, what is the specific act being evaluated (e.g., “kill/redirect/push/harvest”) and what are the relevant consequences beyond the immediate body count (risk, precedent, fear, trust in institutions, future behavior)?

3) “Does it get the cases right?”: Pick one case where your intuition conflicts with the act-consequentialist verdict. What feature seems morally relevant to you that pure outcome-maximizing might miss (e.g., intention vs foresight, using someone as a means, rights/constraints, consent, fairness)?

4) Toward a ‘right general account’: If you think AC misfires, what kind of principle would fix it—an absolute constraint, a conditional constraint (only in emergencies), or a rule about *what kinds* of killing are permissible? Name one principle you’re considering and test it against at least two cases.

Answer these two to start: (i) What’s your one-sentence statement of act consequentialism for this prompt? (ii) Which of Fuel/Blood/trolley do you think is the hardest for act consequentialism, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will use guided discovery and Socratic questioning to help the student generate their own outline rather than supplying one. I’ll keep it bite-sized, focused on the assignment’s cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants), and ask step-by-step questions that move toward: (1) stating act consequentialism’s decision procedure, (2) applying it to each case, and (3) assessing objections and proposing a better general account if needed. I will not write the outline for them; instead I’ll prompt them to fill in each section and justify claims.

exchanges.student:
(i) My one-sentence act consequentialism: an act is right iff, among the available options, it produces the greatest overall expected well-being (or least overall harm) impartially—everyone’s welfare counts equally.

(ii) I think **Blood** is the hardest for act consequentialism, because it seems like it could endorse killing/harvesting one healthy person (or violating consent) to save five, and even if the body count works out, it clashes with my intuition about rights/consent and how that kind of policy would erode trust and create fear (which then feeds back into worse consequences).
exchanges.tutor:
Your (i) is solid and usable.

For Blood, pin it down concretely: what *exactly* are the available options in the scenario as you understand it (list 2–3), and what are the main consequence categories you think an act consequentialist must include (immediate lives saved, long-run trust, fear, institutional incentives, etc.)?

Then a key fork: when you say “it erodes trust and creates fear,” are you treating that as (A) just more consequences to add—so act consequentialism might still condemn harvesting *in practice*—or (B) evidence that we need a non-consequentialist constraint like rights/consent that limits what can be done even when consequences favor it?

To test whether you’re smuggling in a different theory: imagine a super-controlled version where nobody finds out, no trust effects, no future incentives—only one dies, five live. In that cleaned-up case, what would *your* view say, and what would act consequentialism say?

Finally, whichever way you answer, check consistency: does your proposed “fix” also change what you’d say about at least one trolley variant (switch vs push) or Fuel? Which one, and what feature is doing the work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s accurate formulation, then push them to operationalize it in the Blood case with explicit options and consequence categories (including indirect effects like trust). I’ll use Socratic prompts to separate (a) act vs rule consequentialist moves, (b) “include trust in the calculus” vs “introduce deontic constraints,” and (c) test candidate principles across Fuel and trolley to avoid one-off intuition. I will not supply the verdicts; I’ll require the student to state them and defend them.

exchanges.student:
In Blood as I understand it, the options are basically: (1) do nothing and five patients die, (2) kill/harvest organs from one healthy person to save the five, or (3) try to find voluntary donors/alternatives even if it likely fails in time. The consequence buckets I think an act consequentialist has to count include immediate lives saved, long-run trust in doctors/hospitals, fear/avoidance of care, incentives for abuse, and the psychological costs to staff/society.

On the “trust/fear” point, I’m torn but I think it’s mostly (A): extra consequences that often make harvesting wrong in practice; but the super-clean case pushes me toward (B) because even if nobody finds out and it’s strictly 1 vs 5, I still feel it’s wrong due to consent/rights. In that cleaned-up case, act consequentialism seems to say you should harvest (maximize lives), while my view says you still shouldn’t.

If I add a consent/rights constraint as a fix, it would also affect trolley: it would allow “switch” (redirect a threat) more easily than “push” (using someone as a means), so that feature—using a person as an instrument without consent—would do the work across Blood and push-type cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re at the real argumentative crux: your intuitions diverge from AC in the “sanitized” Blood case.

Two moves to make your draft rigorous:

1) Be explicit about AC’s *case verdicts*. For each of these, what does your AC rule imply?
- Blood (sanitized)
- Blood (realistic, with trust/incentives)
- Trolley switch
- Trolley push
- Fuel
Write the verdict as a simple “permissible/required/forbidden” and one reason (in your own words).

2) Turn your “consent/rights constraint” into a testable principle. What is the narrowest version you think you can defend?
- Is it “never kill without consent,” or only “never *use* a person as a means,” or something like “no intentional killing,” or “no violating basic rights except under ____ conditions”?

Now stress-test it: can you name one case where your constraint seems to give the *wrong* result (too strict or too permissive)? For example, does “no killing without consent” accidentally forbid switching the trolley (since the one on the side track didn’t consent)? If so, what refinement would you try?

Last, a precision question you’ll need for Lecture-13-style discussion: what’s the morally relevant difference, in your view, between (a) redirecting an existing threat (switch) and (b) introducing a new threat by using someone (push/harvest)? Try to state that difference without using the word “intuition.”
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will press the student to (1) state the act-consequentialist verdicts explicitly for each case variant, (2) articulate a principled rights/consent constraint in their own terms without me drafting it, and (3) stress-test it across Fuel, Blood, and multiple trolley cases to avoid ad hoc patching. I’ll also guide them to distinguish “using as a means” from “foreseen side-effect” and ask for a counterexample check. I will keep prompts short and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: Can I take a quick break from the cases—what’s a good dinner spot if you’re near MIT/Cambridge, ideally cheap and not too crowded?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t help with restaurant recommendations—my role here is to tutor you on the philosophy assignment.

Let’s get you unstuck quickly: for the *sanitized Blood* case, is killing the one **required**, **permissible**, or **forbidden** on (a) act consequentialism and (b) your emerging rights/consent view? Give one sentence of justification for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must stay in the tutor role and refuse non-academic questions. I will redirect the student back to the assignment with a focused, solution-directed question that advances their progress. I will keep it brief and avoid engaging in the detour.

exchanges.student: Sanitized Blood: on **act consequentialism**, killing the one is **required**, because it uniquely maximizes overall well-being (five survive instead of one). On my emerging **rights/consent** view, it’s **forbidden**, because it violates a basic constraint against killing/using an innocent person without consent even when doing so would produce better aggregate outcomes.
exchanges.tutor:
Clear disagreement—good. Now make your constraint precise enough to survive trolley-style pressure.

1) Does your constraint forbid **trolley switch** too (the person on the side track didn’t consent), or do you think switch is permissible? Which, and what principle explains that difference?

2) When you say “using as a means,” what’s your test: is it about **intention** (you intend their death), **causal role** (their body is part of the plan), or **rights-violation** (you invade/control them)? Pick one.

3) Fuel check: does Fuel look more like “redirecting an existing threat” or more like “creating a new threat/using a person”? Based on that, what verdict does your constraint give—and are you comfortable with it?

Answer (1) first: switch permissible or forbidden on your view, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will push the student to refine the constraint so it’s not ad hoc, and to test it against switch/push and Fuel. I’ll use Socratic questions about intention/means, doing vs allowing, and constraint thresholds, forcing them to articulate a defensible principle and anticipate objections. I’ll keep it brief and avoid supplying wording or full solutions.

exchanges.student: On my view, **trolley switch is permissible**: I’m not attacking or commandeering the one person as a tool; I’m redirecting an already-existing threat and choosing the lesser harm, even though it foreseeably kills someone who didn’t consent. The principle I’m aiming at is something like: *it’s more permissible to redirect/allocate an unavoidable harm than to intentionally kill or physically use an innocent person as part of your plan to save others.*
exchanges.tutor:
You’re close to a defensible “right general account,” but you need sharper criteria.

1) On your principle, what’s your verdict on **trolley push**—and what exact feature flips it from switch (is it physical interference with the person, intending their death, or making their body part of the mechanism)?

2) Apply your principle back to **Blood (sanitized)**: is the wrong-making feature “intentional killing,” “using as a means,” or “violating medical role/authority”? Which is essential?

3) Stress test with a tweak: suppose in switch you *must* grab someone’s hand to pull the lever, briefly using them as leverage, but they don’t die. Does “using” matter only when it’s lethal/rights-violating, or always?

Give me your push verdict + the single most important difference from switch, in one or two sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student sharpen their emerging principle by probing borderline cases and clarifying distinctions (intended vs foreseen, using as means, doing vs allowing). I’ll ask them to apply the principle to push and Blood, then test for counterexamples and whether it collapses into doctrine of double effect or similar. I will not provide formulations; I’ll require the student to supply criteria and verdicts.

exchanges.student: On my principle, **trolley push is forbidden**. The key difference from switch is that in push I **intentionally kill by physically imposing on the person and making their body part of the saving mechanism** (they’re used as the means to stop the trolley), whereas in switch I’m redirecting an existing threat and the death is a foreseen side-effect rather than something I’m doing *to* the person as my plan.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you can *argue* rather than gesture.

Next step: can act consequentialism mimic your verdicts just by “counting more consequences,” or is there a principled gap?

1) For **push**: what would an act consequentialist say if pushing saves five with no long-run bad effects? Would AC require it on your understanding? If yes, what does that show about AC?

2) For **realistic Blood** (with trust/fear): do you think AC condemns harvesting there? If so, does that reduce your objection to AC, or do you still think AC is wrong because of the *sanitized* case?

3) Fuel: where does it land on your intention/means test—does it look more like switch (side-effect) or like push/Blood (means)? State your tentative verdict.

Answer (1) and (2) briefly: does AC still conflict with you even after adding indirect consequences, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will consolidate the student’s distinctions and push them to connect them explicitly to evaluating act consequentialism and to the remaining cases (Fuel, realistic Blood). I’ll ask them to anticipate an act-consequentialist reply and to articulate whether their principle is best framed as a constraint, a threshold, or a different consequentialist variant. I’ll keep it short and require the student to supply the argumentative steps.

exchanges.student: Even after adding indirect consequences, I think AC still conflicts with me because in the “no long-run bad effects” version it would still require pushing (and sanitized Blood) to save five, while my view forbids it due to the means/intentional-killing constraint. In realistic Blood, AC might condemn harvesting once you include trust/fear/incentives, but that doesn’t really answer my objection since the sanitized case isolates what I think is a principled limit AC can’t capture.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a coherent critique: AC can’t respect a genuine constraint if the sanitized case is possible.

To finish your prompt, you now need a clean “general account” + a quick case sweep.

1) State your proposed account as a *general rule* (no hedging): what kinds of killing are ruled out even to save five, and what kinds (if any) are allowed?

2) Give one justification that isn’t just “rights”: what’s the moral rationale—respect for persons, agency, not treating people as tools, etc.?

3) Run the rule across the set:
- Blood (sanitized)
- Blood (realistic)
- Switch
- Push
- Fuel
For each, does it deliver the verdict you want? If one case comes out “wrong,” which refinement would you make?

Write your rule in 1–2 sentences (your own words), then list the five case verdicts as P/F (permissible/forbidden).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will guide the student to articulate the evaluation section: why AC seems compelling, where it misfires, and the student’s proposed alternative account. I’ll prompt them to formulate their account as a general rule with a rationale and to test it across all required cases (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants). I will not draft prose; I’ll ask for a structured set of claims and checks, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student:
**Rule (1–2 sentences):** It’s forbidden to intentionally kill an innocent person or to use their body/agency as a means to saving others (even to save five). It can be permissible to *redirect or allocate an already-existing, unavoidable threat* so as to minimize harm, when the victim’s death is a foreseen side-effect rather than part of the plan.

**Rationale (brief):** This treats people as ends with their own agency, not as tools to be deployed for aggregate benefit; it limits what can be justified “for the greater good.”

**Case verdicts (P/F):**
- **Blood (sanitized): F**
- **Blood (realistic): F**
- **Trolley switch: P**
- **Trolley push: F**
- **Fuel: P** (tentatively, insofar as it’s closer to redirecting/choosing the lesser harm than to harvesting/using someone as a mechanism)
exchanges.tutor:
This is a strong start, but it’s still vulnerable to “definition fiddling.” Tighten two pressure points:

1) “Already-existing, unavoidable threat”: unavoidable *for whom*? If you could avoid the threat by doing nothing (letting five die), does that make it “avoidable” and undermine switch/Fuel? How will you state this without circularity?

2) “Intentionally kill” vs “foreseen side-effect”: in switch, you *choose* a track knowing the person will die. Why isn’t that “intentional” on your test? What’s your criterion for intention (aim/plan vs knowledge)?

Now do one robustness check: invent (or recall) one variant where your rule gives a result you dislike (even a little). What tweak would fix it without breaking your other verdicts?

Finally, to match the prompt: according to your view, when—if ever—is it morally okay to kill one to save five? Give one condition where it’s allowed, or say “never,” and defend that choice.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give targeted critical feedback by probing ambiguities (what counts as “intentional,” “means,” “already-existing threat,” “unavoidable”) and asking for one hard counterexample and a refinement. I’ll also make them explicitly answer the assignment’s question about when it’s morally okay to kill one to save five, and to position their view relative to act consequentialism. I will keep it short and not provide rewritten language.

exchanges.student:
I’m stuck on tightening my rule: by “already-existing, unavoidable threat” I mean a threat that will kill someone no matter what I do (I’m only choosing who it hits), but I’m not sure how to say that without it sounding circular since “doing nothing” avoids killing the one but lets the five die. Also, for intention: I want “intended” to mean part of my aim/plan (the death is a means), not merely something I knowingly allow as a side-effect—does that distinction actually hold up in switch?

A variant that worries me is: what if the only way to pull the switch is to shove someone onto the lever platform (they don’t die), which feels like “using” them but maybe still permissible—so maybe the constraint should be about *lethal* use/intentional killing specifically. And to answer the prompt, I think it’s morally okay to kill one to save five only in something like self-defense/defense-of-others where the one is an unjust aggressor, or possibly in a true “least-harm” redirection case where their death isn’t intended as a means.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—these are the right worries.

To avoid circularity, try separating **(a) what the threat will do if you don’t intervene** from **(b) what your intervention changes**.
- In switch-style cases, what is the “default” trajectory of harm *independent of you*?
- What exactly does your action change: does it **introduce** a new victim, or does it **re-route** an existing harmful process?

Can you state “unavoidable” as: “Given the threat’s default path, at least one person will be killed, and every available intervention results in someone being killed”? Would that capture your thought without appealing to ‘who you choose’?

On intention: use a test. Ask yourself in switch: *If the one person were not on the side track, would your plan still work and would you still do it?* In push/Blood: *If the one person weren’t there / weren’t usable, would your plan fail?* How do those answers map onto “means” vs “side-effect” for you?

Now integrate your aggressor idea: does your rule need a separate clause like “unless the person is an unjust aggressor/liable”? If yes, what makes someone “liable” in a way that a random bystander in trolley is not?

Answer just these:
1) What is the default (no-intervention) outcome in your Fuel case as you understand it?
2) Using the “would the plan still work if the victim weren’t there?” test, what’s the result for switch vs push?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student resolve circularity by distinguishing baseline facts from agent-caused interventions, and by using guided questions to refine “unavoidable” into a non-circular structure (fixed threat trajectory, forced choice, limited control). I’ll probe the intention/means vs side-effect distinction with a concrete test and ask them to apply it to switch/Fuel. I’ll also push them to incorporate the aggressor/self-defense condition and to reconcile it with their earlier rule. I will not supply final wording, only questions and a small conceptual scaffold.

deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor consistently enforces a Socratic approach and avoids giving answers, but never explains to the student *why* it's doing so (e.g., the rationale that active reasoning builds long-term philosophical skill, or that doing the work for the student would undermine learning). The tutor simply refuses off-task requests or redirects without explaining its pedagogical policy.

In all three transcripts, the tutor never prompts the student to reflect on how their thinking changed or what method worked. For example, in T1 after the student successfully develops the means/side-effect distinction and a 2x2 grid, the tutor moves to the next stress-test without asking the student to articulate what they learned or why that approach worked. Similarly in T3, after the student articulates a full rule and verdicts, no reflection prompt is offered.

Across all three transcripts, the tutor repeatedly sends long multi-part responses (4–6 numbered sub-questions at once) when shorter, more focused single prompts would give the student more room to think. For example, T1 Turn 2 asks three distinct analytical tasks simultaneously; T1 Turn 4 has three probes; T2 Turn 2 has four questions; T3 Turn 1 has four structured sub-tasks. This pattern is consistent throughout and reduces the student's opportunity to respond step by step.

overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor performs strongly on core Socratic method (1.1): it never gives answers, consistently redirects off-topic requests, uses probing questions to develop the student's reasoning, and builds directly on student progress. No deductions were warranted for 1.1 or 1.2.

The main weaknesses are in meta-learning (1.3) and response length (3.1). The tutor never explains its own pedagogical rationale (why it won't answer directly), and it never invites the student to reflect on what worked or how their thinking changed—both missed opportunities for deeper learning.

Response length is a consistent issue: the tutor routinely asks 3–6 sub-questions per turn. While the questions are individually good, the cumulative weight reduces interactive discovery and gives students less space to think. A more targeted one-question-at-a-time approach would better serve the Socratic method the tutor otherwise employs well.

Dialogue quality (2.1, 2.2) and tone (3.2) are handled well throughout: the tutor stays on-task, redirects chitchat cleanly, advances the conversation without spiraling, and maintains an encouraging but appropriately challenging tone.
